verified_title.en: Arabic Word Types: Noun (Ism), Verb (Fiʿl), and Particle (Ḥarf)
verified_title.tr: Arapçada Kelime Çeşitleri: İsim, Fiil ve Harf

L1W1.summary: This lesson introduces the three fundamental categories of Arabic words: the noun (اسم), the verb (فعل), and the particle (حرف). Each category is defined by its meaning and grammatical behaviour — nouns denote meaning independent of time, verbs denote an action tied to time, and particles only yield meaning in combination with other words. The lesson then provides Quranic examples and practice exercises for identifying each word type.

name.en: The Three Divisions of the Arabic Word

verified_title.en: Definite and Indefinite Nouns, and Solar & Lunar Letters
verified_title.tr: Belirli ve Belirsiz İsimler ile Şemsî ve Kamerî Harfler

L1W2.summary: This lesson introduces the distinction between definite nouns (ma'rifa) and indefinite nouns (nakira) in Arabic, explaining that definiteness is marked by the article ال and the absence of tanwin, while indefiniteness is marked by tanwin. The lesson also covers solar letters (al-huruf al-shamsiyya), where the lam of ال assimilates to the following letter, and lunar letters (al-huruf al-qamariyya), where the lam is pronounced clearly. Students practice identifying and converting between definite and indefinite forms using vocabulary from short Quranic suras.

name.en: Tanwin as a Marker of Indefiniteness

verified_title.en: Masculine & Feminine Nouns and Demonstrative Pronouns
verified_title.tr: Eril ve Dişil İsimler ve İşaret İsimleri

L1W3.summary: This lesson introduces the Arabic grammatical distinction between masculine (مذكر) and feminine (مؤنث) nouns, explaining that a noun's gender is determined by which demonstrative pronoun (هذا or هذه) correctly points to it. It then covers the signs of femininity including tā' marbūṭa (ة), maqṣūra alif (ى/ا), and mamdūda alif (اء), with examples from the Quran. Finally, the lesson presents the full system of demonstrative pronouns (أسماء الإشارة) covering near and far, singular and plural, masculine and feminine, illustrated with Quranic verses.

name.en: Maqṣūra and Mamdūda Alif as Feminine Markers
name.tr: Maksure ve Memdude Elif — Dişillik Alâmeti

current_title.tr: Eril ve Dişil İsimler / İşaret İsimleri

verified_title.en: Idafa (Genitive / Possessive Noun Construction)
verified_title.tr: İsim Tamlaması (İzafet)

L1W4.summary: This lesson introduces the Arabic genitive construction (الإضافة), explaining that it is a relationship between two nouns where the first (مضاف) loses its nunation (tanwin) and the second (مضاف إليه) is always in the genitive case (majrur). Students practise forming idafa phrases from pairs of nouns and then identify idafa constructions within short Quranic surahs (al-Maun, al-Fatiha, al-Fil, Quraysh, al-Kawthar, al-Kafirun, al-Nasr, al-Masad, al-Falaq, al-Nas, al-Baqara, al-Hashr).

name.en: Mudaf ilayhi (Second noun in idafa, genitive)
Dropping of tanwin on the mudaf

verified_title.en: Adjective and the Noun it Describes (Al-Sifa and Al-Mawsuf)
verified_title.tr: Sıfat ve Mevsuf (Nitelenen İsim)

L1W5.summary: This lesson introduces the Arabic adjective (al-sifa) and the noun it qualifies (al-mawsuf). It teaches that the adjective must agree with its noun in four categories: gender (masculine/feminine), number (singular/dual/plural), definiteness (definite/indefinite), and case ending (i'rab). The lesson also covers the special rule that the non-rational plural is treated grammatically as a feminine singular, and includes practice identifying adjective-noun pairs from Quranic verses.

name.en: Adjective and Described Noun (Na't and Man'ut)

name.ar: جَمْعُ غَيْرِ الْعَاقِلِ يُعَامَلُ مُعَامَلَةَ الْمُفْرَدِ الْمُؤَنَّث
name.en: Non-Rational Plural Treated as Feminine Singular
name.tr: Akılsız Varlıkların Çoğulu Müfred Müennes Gibi Kabul Edilir

verified_title.en: Pronouns (Detached/Separate Pronouns for Nominative and Accusative)
verified_title.tr: Zamirler (Munfasıl Ref' ve Nasb Zamirleri)

L1W6.summary: This lesson introduces Arabic pronouns (ضمائر), defined as nouns indicating a hidden/implied referent. It covers the full paradigm of detached nominative pronouns (ضمائر رفع منفصلة) including singular, dual, and plural forms for all persons and genders, illustrated with Quranic examples. It then introduces detached accusative pronouns (ضمائر نصب منفصلة) such as إيّاي and إيّاك, also illustrated with Quranic verses.

name.en: Detached Pronoun as Subject in Nominal Sentence

verified_title.en: Attached Pronouns (Damair Muttasila)
verified_title.tr: Bitişik Zamirler (Muttasıl Zamirler)

L1W7.summary: This lesson introduces the attached (suffixed) pronouns in Arabic, showing how they attach to verbs, nouns, and prepositions for all persons, genders, and numbers. It presents paradigms using the verb كَتَبَ, the noun كِتَاب, and prepositions such as مَعَ and لِ, demonstrating how the same pronoun suffix changes the vowel depending on grammatical case context. Students then practice identifying attached pronouns in Quranic verses.

name.en: Identifying Attached Pronouns in Quranic Verses

verified_title.en: Prepositions (Huruf al-Jarr) and Adverbs of Place (Zarf al-Makan)
verified_title.tr: Harf-i Cerler ve Yer Zarfları

L1W8.summary: This lesson introduces the Arabic prepositions (حروف الجر) and explains that they attach to nouns and cause them to take the genitive case (majrur). The lesson lists the main prepositions (من، إلى، حتى، في، عن، على، اللام، واو القسم، تاء القسم، الكاف، الباء) with examples from short Quranic surahs. It also introduces adverbs of place (ظروف المكان) such as فوق، تحت، خلف، أمام، وراء، بين، حول with Quranic examples illustrating their usage.

name.en: Oath Prepositions: Waw and Ta of Qasam

verified_title.en: Nominal Sentence (Jumlah Ismiyya): Mubtada and Khabar
verified_title.tr: İsim Cümlesi: Mübteda ve Haber

L1W9.summary: This lesson introduces the nominal sentence (jumlah ismiyya) as a sentence beginning with a noun, contrasted with the verbal sentence (jumlah fi'liyya). It explains the mubtada (subject) as a definite, marfu' noun and the khabar (predicate) as a marfu' word or phrase that completes the meaning. The lesson also covers agreement rules between mubtada and khabar in gender, number, and the special treatment of non-rational plurals as feminine singular.

name.en: Agreement of Khabar with Mubtada (Gender and Number)

name.ar: جمع غير العاقل يُعامَل معاملة المفرد المؤنث
name.en: Non-Rational Plural Treated as Feminine Singular
name.tr: Akılsız Çoğul, Müfred Müennes Gibi Kabul Edilir

verified_title.en: Singular, Dual, and Sound Masculine Plural
verified_title.tr: Tekil, İkil ve Kurallı Eril Çoğul

L1W10.summary: This lesson introduces the three number categories in Arabic grammar: singular (مفرد), dual (مثنى), and plural (جمع), with a focus on the sound masculine plural (جمع مذكر سالم). It explains how masculine nouns and adjectives form the dual by adding ان/ين and the sound masculine plural by adding ون/ين, demonstrating paradigms with multiple Quranic vocabulary items. Students then practice identifying sound masculine plurals in Quranic verses.

current_title.en: Singular, Dual, Plural · Sound Masculine Plural
current_title.tr: Tekil, İkil, Çoğul · Kurallı Muzekker Çoğul
current_title.ar: المفرد والمثنى والجمع · جمع المذكر السالم

verified_title.en: Sound Feminine Plural (Jam' Mu'annath Salim)
verified_title.tr: Kurallı Müennes Çoğul (Cem' Müennes Salim)

L1W11.summary: This lesson introduces the sound feminine plural (jam' mu'annath salim), explaining that a plural denotes more than two and is formed by adding ـَاتٌ (nominative) or ـَاتٍ (genitive/accusative) to the feminine singular base. A paradigm table shows the singular, dual, and sound feminine plural forms for common Quranic adjectives and nouns. The lesson concludes with Quranic identification exercises drawing from Surat al-Ahzab 35, al-Tawbah 72, al-Baqarah 25/238, and others.

name.en: Case Endings of the Sound Feminine Plural
Sound Feminine Plural in Quranic Context

verified_title.en: Broken Plural (Jam' al-Taksir) — Patterns and Quranic Examples
verified_title.tr: Kırık Çoğul (Cemü't-Teksir) — Kalıpları ve Kuran'dan Örnekler

L1W12.summary: This lesson introduces the broken plural (Jam' al-Taksir) in Arabic, defining it as a plural form that denotes more than two and involves an internal change to the singular's structure. It presents the major broken plural patterns (e.g. فِعَال، فُعُل، أَفْعَال، فُعُول، أَفْعِلَة، أَفْعُل، فُعَلاء، أَفْعِلاء) with paired singular-plural examples. The lesson then reinforces these patterns through identification exercises using Quranic verses.

name.en: Broken Plural Patterns: fu'alā', af'ilā', af'ila, af'ul
name.tr: Kırık Çoğul Kalıpları: fuʿalāʾ, afʿilāʾ, afʿila, afʿul

verified_title.en: The Past Tense Verb (Al-Fi'l Al-Madi) — Forms and Conjugations
verified_title.tr: Geçmiş Zaman Fiili (El-Fi'lu'l-Mâdî) — Çekimleri

L1W13.summary: This lesson introduces the Arabic past tense verb (الفعل الماضي), presenting its base forms (فَعَلَ, كَتَبَ, رَفَعَ, سَبَّحَ, عَلَّمَ, قَالَ) and their full conjugation across all pronouns (أنا, نحن, أنتَ, أنتِ, أنتم, أنتنّ, هو, هي, هم, هنّ). It also introduces the concept of using the past tense form to express certain future meanings (مُحَقَّق الوقوع), where an event is so certain it is described as if already past. Numerous Quranic examples are provided to illustrate both the conjugation patterns and the identification of past tense verbs in context.

name.en: Conjugation of the Past Tense Verb
Past Tense Verb Identification in Quran

verified_title.en: The Present Tense Verb (Al-Fi'l Al-Mudari')
verified_title.tr: Geniş Zaman Fiili (Muzari' Fiil)

L1W14.summary: This lesson introduces the Arabic present tense verb (al-fi'l al-mudāri') by presenting a full conjugation table across all pronouns (first, second, and third person, singular and plural, masculine and feminine) using common Quranic verbs. Students practice identifying mudāri' verbs through exercises drawn directly from short Quranic suras (al-Fātiha, al-Fīl, al-Māʿūn, al-Kāfirūn, al-Nasr, al-Nās, al-Baqara, etc.). The lesson also opens and closes with a supplication (duʿāʾ) that is rich in first-person plural mudāri' verb forms.

name.en: Identifying Mudari' Verbs in Quranic Verses
Feminine Singular Mudari' with Ta-prefix and Feminine Marker

verified_title.en: The Imperative: Direct Command (Amr), Lam al-Amr (Third-Person Command), Amma, and Lam al-Ibtida'
verified_title.tr: Emir Fiili: Doğrudan Emir (Emr-i Hâzır), Lâmu'l-Emir (Emr-i Gâib), Emmâ ve Lâmu'l-İbtidâ
verified_title.ar: الأمر: فعل الأمر، لام الأمر، أمَّا، ولام الابتداء

L2W1.summary: This lesson covers three related Arabic grammar topics: (1) the imperative verb (فعل الأمر) formed directly for the second person across all conjugations; (2) lam al-amr (لام الأمر), which uses the prefixed lam to form third-person and first-person commands with the jussive (majzum) mudari'; (3) the particle أمَّا (a conditional-emphatic particle whose apodosis is introduced by fa-); and (4) lam al-ibtida' (لام الابتداء), an emphatic lam that enters on nouns and imperfect verbs to affirm the content of a sentence. Quranic examples from Surah al-Duha, al-Baqara, al-Nas, al-Kawthar, and others are used throughout.

current_title.tr: Emr-i Hazır/Emr-i Gaib · لاَ · Lâm Al-İbtida

verified_title.en: Prohibition (Nahiy) and Active Participle (Ism al-Fa'il)
verified_title.tr: Nehiy ve İsm-i Fâil

L2W2.summary: This lesson introduces the Arabic prohibition form (nahiy), explaining that it is formed by prefixing the prohibitive لا (lā al-nāhiya) to the present tense (muḍāriʿ) verb, causing it to be jussive (majzūm). It covers the conjugation of prohibition across all relevant pronouns, including defective verbs and the five verb forms (al-afʿāl al-khamsa), with Quranic examples. The second half introduces the active participle (ism al-fāʿil), showing its derivation pattern (fāʿilun for triliteral verbs; muXXiʿun for non-triliteral), and its declension in singular, dual, and plural forms, again with Quranic examples.

examples: لا الناهية + الفعل المضارع = لا تَفْعَلْ

verified_title.en: Case Endings (I'rab) for Nouns
verified_title.tr: İsimlerde İ'rab (Hâl Ekleri)

L2W3.summary: This lesson introduces the three primary case endings (i'rab) for Arabic nouns: raf' (nominative, marked by damma), nasb (accusative, marked by fatha), and jarr (genitive, marked by kasra). It presents a comprehensive declension table covering singular, dual, sound masculine plural, sound feminine plural, and broken plural forms across all three cases. The lesson concludes with exercises identifying case endings in Quranic verses.

examples: لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ

verified_title.en: Verbal Sentence (Jumlah Fi'liyya) and Intensive Adjective Forms (Siyagh al-Mubalaghah)
verified_title.tr: Fiil Cümlesi ve Mübalağa Sıfatları

L2W4.summary: This lesson introduces the verbal sentence (jumlah fi'liyya), defined as a sentence beginning with a verb, and explains its three constituents: the verb (fi'l), the subject (fa'il), and the object (maf'ul). It also covers verb conjugation across all pronouns in past, present, and imperative forms, and distinguishes verbal sentences from nominal sentences. Additionally, the lesson introduces intensive adjective forms (siyagh al-mubalaghah), derived nouns indicating frequency or intensity of an action, presenting the main patterns (fa''al, mif'al, fa'ul, fa'il, fi'il, fu'alah) with Quranic examples.

name.tr: İsim Cümlesi ve Fiil Cümlesi Karşılaştırması

verified_title.en: Types of مَا / Future Tense Particles (س and سوف)
verified_title.tr: مَا'nın Çeşitleri / Gelecek Zaman Edatları (س ve سوف)

L2W5.summary: This lesson covers the seven types of the Arabic particle مَا (interrogative, relative, negating, masdariyya, exclamatory, conditional, and kaffa), illustrating each with Quranic examples. It then introduces the two future tense particles س (for near future) and سوف (for distant future), both of which precede the present-tense verb (المضارع). Students practice identifying both phenomena across a wide range of Quranic verses.

name.en: Future Tense Particles: س (near future) and سوف (distant future)
name.tr: Gelecek Zaman Edatları: س (yakın) ve سوف (uzak)

current_title.en: Types of لا / Future Tense (سوف / س)
current_title.ar: أنواع لا / المستقبل بـ سوف وس

verified_title.en: Negation Particles: لَيْسَ, مَا, and لَا (Negating Verbal and Nominal Sentences)
verified_title.tr: Olumsuzluk Edatları: لَيْسَ / مَا / لَا ile Fiil ve İsim Cümlelerinin Olumsuzlanması
verified_title.ar: أدوات النفي: لَيْسَ وَمَا وَلَا في الجملة الفعلية والاسمية

L2W6.summary: This lesson teaches how verbal sentences (الجملة الفعلية) are negated using مَا (typically with past-tense verbs) and لَا (typically with present-tense verbs), and how nominal sentences (الجملة الاسمية) are negated using لَيْسَ, مَا, or لَا. It explains that لَيْسَ, as a sister of كَانَ, keeps the subject (مبتدأ) in the nominative (مرفوع) while putting the predicate (خبر) in the accusative (منصوب). Quranic examples from multiple surahs are used to practice identifying negated verbs and nouns.

name.ar: النفي بـ مَا في الجملة الفعلية
name.en: Negation of verbal sentences with مَا

examples: وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ
مَا جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لِرَجُلٍ مِنْ قَلْبَيْنِ

name.ar: النفي بـ لَا في الجملة الفعلية
name.en: Negation of verbal sentences with لَا

examples: قُلْ لَا أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِي نَفْعًا وَلَا ضَرًّا

name.ar: النفي بـ لَيْسَ في الجملة الاسمية
name.en: Negation of nominal sentences with لَيْسَ
Conjugation of لَيْسَ for all pronouns

name.ar: النفي بـ مَا في الجملة الاسمية
name.en: Negation of nominal sentences with مَا

name.ar: النفي بـ لَا النافية للجنس في الجملة الاسمية
name.en: Negation of nominal sentences with لَا (lā nāfiya lil-jins)
name.tr: İsim cümlelerinin لَا ile olumsuzlanması (cins olumsuzlama)

verified_title.en: Particles That Put the Muḍāriʿ (Present/Future) Verb in the Subjunctive (Manṣūb) Case
verified_title.tr: Geniş Zaman Fiilini Mansûb Yapan Edatlar (Nâsıblar)

L2W7.summary: This lesson covers the particles (ḥurūf al-naṣb) that cause the imperfect/present verb (fiʿl al-muḍāriʿ) to take the manṣūb (subjunctive) form. The particles treated are: أَنْ, لَنْ, كَيْ, لِـ (lām al-taʿlīl), لِـ (lām al-juḥūd), فَاء al-sabābiyya, حَتَّى, and إِذَنْ, each with its grammatical meaning and conditions. The lesson also shows how the five special verb forms (al-afʿāl al-khamsa) and defective (muʿtall) verbs are affected, with extensive Quranic examples for identification practice.

name.en: Al-Afʿāl al-Khamsa in the manṣūb (subjunctive) — deletion of nūn
name.tr: Beş Fiilin Mansûb Hali — Nûn'un Düşmesi

current_title.en: Subjunctive Mood — Mansub Form of Present Tense

verified_title.en: Jussive Mood (Majzum): Particles That Govern It and Their Effects on the Mudari' Verb
verified_title.tr: Cezm Eden Edatlar ve Muzari Fiilin Meczum Hali

L2W8.summary: This lesson teaches the jussive mood (majzum) of the Arabic imperfect verb (mudari'), focusing on the four particles that cause jussive: لَمْ, لَمَّا, لام الأمر, and لا الناهية. It explains the signs of jussive (dropping of nun in the five verb forms, dropping of weak letter in defective verbs, sukun in sound verbs) and distinguishes the three different uses of لَمَّا (as a jussive particle, as a temporal conjunction with past tense, and as a particle meaning 'except'). Extensive Quranic examples are provided for identifying jussive verbs in context.

keywords: harf nafy wa jazm wa qalb

name.en: Lamma — Jussive Particle / Temporal Conjunction / Exception Particle
name.tr: Lemmâ — Cezm Edatı / Zaman Zarfı / İstisna Edatı

examples: إِنْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ لَمَّا عَلَيْهَا حَافِظٌ

name.ar: علامات الجزم في الأفعال الخمسة والأفعال المعتلة
name.en: Signs of Jussive: Dropping of Nun (Five Verbs) and Weak Letter (Defective Verbs)
name.tr: Meczumun Alametleri: Beş Fiilde Nun'un ve İlletli Fiillerde İllet Harfinin Düşmesi

current_title.en: Jussive Mood — Majzum Form of Present Tense

verified_title.en: Passive Voice: Passive Verb (Fi'l al-Mabni lil-Majhul) and Passive Participle (Ism al-Maf'ul)
verified_title.tr: Edilgen Çatı: Meçhul Fiil ve İsm-i Mef'ul

L2W9.summary: This lesson introduces the passive voice in Arabic (الفعل المبني للمجهول), explaining how to form the passive of past and present tense verbs across various verb types (sound, doubled, hollow, defective, Form IV, Form X). It then presents the passive participle (اسم المفعول), derived on the pattern مَفْعُول for trilateral verbs and مُـ...َـل for non-trilateral verbs, contrasting it with the active participle. Numerous Quranic examples are provided for identification and analysis of both passive verbs and passive participles.

name.ar: الفعل المبني للمعلوم مقابل المبني للمجهول
name.tr: Malum Fiil ile Meçhul Fiil Karşılaştırması

verified_title.en: إِنَّ and its sisters, كَانَ and its sisters, إِذْ, إِذَا, and لَمَّا
verified_title.tr: İnne ve kardeşleri, Kâne ve kardeşleri, İz, İzâ ve Lemmâ
verified_title.ar: إِنَّ وأخواتها وكَانَ وأخواتها وإِذْ وإِذَا ولَمَّا

L2W10.summary: This lesson teaches the grammar of إِنَّ (and its sisters), which put the mubtada into the accusative and leave the khabar in the nominative, contrasted with كَانَ (and its sisters), which keep the mubtada nominative and put the khabar in the accusative. It also covers the temporal adverbs إِذْ (past time, reason, surprise) and إِذَا (future/conditional, surprise), along with the three usages of لَمَّا: as a non-jussive conditional time-adverb with a past verb, as a negating particle with a present verb, and as a restrictive particle meaning 'except'. Numerous Quranic examples illustrate each construction.

name.en: Inna and its sisters (particles that govern the nominal sentence)
Kana and its sisters (defective verbs governing the nominal sentence)
Mubtada and Khabar (subject and predicate of the nominal sentence)
Lamma (temporal conjunction, negation particle, or restrictive particle)
name.tr: Lemmâ (zaman zarfı, nefy veya hasr edatı)

verified_title.en: Inna and Its Sisters / Possessive Particles (Dhū and Its Forms) / Noun Declension
verified_title.tr: İnne ve Kardeşleri / Sahiplik Edatları (Zū ve Çekimleri) / İsim Çekimi
verified_title.ar: إِنَّ وأخواتها / أسماء الملكية (ذو وتصريفاتها) / إعراب الأسماء

L2W11.summary: This lesson covers the six sisters of inna (إِنَّ، أَنَّ، لَكِنَّ، كَأَنَّ، لَيْتَ، لَعَلَّ), their meanings and grammatical rule of putting the mubtada into the nasb case and leaving the khabar in the raf state. It then introduces the possessive particle dhū (ذُو) and its full declension by gender, number, and case, alongside the five special nouns (الأسماء الخمسة). Finally, a comprehensive noun declension table is presented covering singular, dual, sound masculine plural, sound feminine plural, and broken plural across all three cases.

verified_title.en: Kāna and Its Sisters / Restriction with إِنْ...إِلَّا and مَا...إِلَّا / Conjunctions وَ، فَ، ثُمَّ
verified_title.tr: Kâne ve Kardeşleri / İn...İllâ ve Mâ...İllâ ile Hasır / Bağlaçlar وَ، فَ، ثُمَّ
verified_title.ar: كَانَ وَأَخَوَاتُهَا / الْحَصْرُ بِإِنْ...إِلَّا وَمَا...إِلَّا / حُرُوفُ الْعَطْفِ وَ، فَ، ثُمَّ

L2W12.summary: This lesson introduces كَانَ and its sisters (أَصْبَحَ، أَمْسَى، أَضْحَى، ظَلَّ، بَاتَ، صَارَ، لَيْسَ), explaining that they raise the mubtada' (nominative subject) and put the khabar in the accusative (mansub). The lesson also covers restriction constructions using إِنْ...إِلَّا, مَا...إِلَّا, and لَا...إِلَّا to express exclusivity. Additionally, the Arabic conjunctions وَ (simple joining), فَ (immediate sequence), and ثُمَّ (delayed sequence) are contrasted with Quranic examples.

verified_title.en: Question Particles, Plural Idafa (Sound Masculine Plural in Genitive Construction), and Vocative Particles (Nida)
verified_title.tr: Soru Edatları, Çoğul İsim Tamlamaları (Eril Çoğul İdafe) ve Nida (Seslenme) Edatları
verified_title.ar: أدوات الاستفهام وإضافة جمع المذكر السالم وأسلوب النداء

L2W13.summary: This lesson covers three major topics: (1) Arabic interrogative particles (أَ، هَل، مَا، مَاذَا، مَن، مَتَى، أَيْنَ، كَيْفَ، كَم، أَيّ، أَنَّى) with their specific usages and Quranic examples; (2) the genitive/idafa construction using the sound masculine plural, where the final nun is dropped when the plural is used as mudaf (e.g. مُسْلِمُو الْيَوْمِ, ظَالِمِي الْقَوْمِ); (3) vocative particles (النداء) distinguishing between particles for near and distant addressees (أَ، أَيْ، أَيَا، هَيَا، آ، يَا) including the special construction يَا أَيُّهَا with a definite noun.

name.tr: Eril Sağlam Çoğulun İsim Tamlamasında Kullanımı (Nun Düşmesi)

name.en: Vocative with Definite (al-) Noun via ayyuha

verified_title.en: Comparative/Superlative (Ism al-Tafdil) and Exclamatory Expression (Uslub al-Ta'ajjub)
verified_title.tr: İsm-i Tafdil ve Ta'accub Üslubu

L2W14.summary: This lesson teaches the formation and usage of ism al-tafdil (the comparative/superlative noun) on the pattern أَفْعَلُ, including its conditions, feminine form (فُعْلَى), and four usage cases (bare, with ال, annexed to indefinite, annexed to definite). It also covers the two standard Arabic exclamatory constructions: مَا أَفْعَلَهُ and أَفْعِلْ بِهِ, with their conditions and Quranic examples. Special irregular comparative words (خَيْر، شَرّ) and the method of forming comparatives from non-triliteral verbs using a helper word plus accusative masdar are also explained.

name.en: Conditions for Forming Ism al-Tafdil on أَفْعَلُ
Feminine Form of Ism al-Tafdil (Fu'la pattern)

name.ar: أحوال اسم التفضيل (مجرد، مع ال، مضاف)
name.en: Four Usage States of Ism al-Tafdil

name.ar: اسم التفضيل من الفعل غير الثلاثي
name.en: Comparative from Non-Triliteral Verbs (with accusative masdar)

verified_title.en: Types of Verbal Nouns (Masdar)
verified_title.tr: Masdar Çeşitleri

L3W1.summary: This lesson presents a comprehensive overview of the Arabic verbal noun (masdar) and its many types. It covers the standard masdar patterns for triliteral and derived verb forms, as well as special categories including the mimated masdar (masdar mimi), the artificial masdar (masdar sinai), the interpreted masdar (masdar mu'awwal), the noun of masdar (ism al-masdar), the masdar of once (masdar al-marra), and the masdar of manner (masdar al-hay'a). Quranic examples are provided throughout to illustrate each type in context.

name.en: Masdar of Once (Marra) and Masdar of Manner (Hay'a)

verified_title.en: Nouns of Time and Place, Instrument Nouns, Attributive Adjective (Nisba), Diminutive, and Similative Participle
verified_title.tr: İsmi Zaman ve Mekân, İsmi Âlet, İsmi Mensûb, Tasgîr ve Sıfat-ı Müşebbehe
verified_title.ar: اسم الزمان والمكان، اسم الآلة، الاسم المنسوب، التصغير، والصفة المشبهة

L3W2.summary: This lesson covers five derived noun categories in Arabic: nouns of time and place (on the patterns مَفْعَل / مَفْعِل from triliteral roots, and on the pattern of the passive participle from augmented roots), instrument nouns (on the patterns مِفْعَال / مِفْعَل / مِفْعَلة from triliteral verbs), attributive adjectives formed by adding a shaddah yā (nisba), diminutive nouns (on the patterns فُعَيْل / فُعَيْعِل / فُعَيْعِيل), and the similative participle (الصفة المشبهة) denoting stable qualities. Each category is illustrated with paradigm tables and Quranic examples.

name.en: Noun of Time and Place (Ism al-Zaman wa al-Makan)

current_title.en: Noun of Time · Place · Instrument · Attribution · Diminutive
current_title.tr: İsmi Zaman · Mekân · Âlet · Mensûb · Tasgîr
current_title.ar: اسم الزمان والمكان والآلة والمنسوب والتصغير

verified_title.en: Introduction to Defective Verbs (Fi'l Mu'tall) — Classification of Sound and Weak Verbs
verified_title.tr: İlletli Fiillere Giriş — Sahih ve Mu'tell Fiillerin Sınıflandırılması
verified_title.ar: مقدمة في الفعل المعتل — تصنيف الأفعال الصحيحة والمعتلة

L3W3.summary: This lesson introduces the classification of Arabic verbs into sound (صحيح) and weak/defective (معتل) categories. Sound verbs are subdivided into sālim, mahmūz, and muḍaʿʿaf, while weak verbs are subdivided into mithāl, ajwaf, nāqiṣ, and lafīf based on which root letter is a weak letter (alif, wāw, or yāʾ). Numerous Quranic examples illustrate each verb type, and the six patterns of the bare triliteral verb (فَعَلَ يَفْعُلُ etc.) are also presented.

name.en: Weak Verb — Mithāl (first radical is weak)
Weak Verb — Ajwaf (middle radical is weak)
Weak Verb — Nāqiṣ (final radical weak) and Lafīf (two weak radicals)

current_title.en: Resembling Adjective (Sifat Mushabbaha) / Defective Verbs — Introduction
current_title.ar: الصفة المشبهة / مدخل إلى الأفعال المعتلة

verified_title.en: Conjugation of Defective Verbs (Past Tense) — All Weak Verb Types
verified_title.tr: İlletli Fiillerin Çekimi (Mazi) — Tüm Zayıf Fiil Türleri
verified_title.ar: تصريف الأفعال المعتلة في الماضي — جميع الأنواع

L3W4.summary: This lesson presents a comprehensive conjugation table of the Arabic past tense (māḍī) across all major verb categories: the sound verb (sālim), the hamzated verb (mahmūz), the doubled verb (muḍaʿʿaf), the assimilated verb (mithāl), the hollow verb (ajwaf), the defective verb (nāqiṣ), and the doubly weak verb (lafīf). Multiple Quranic examples are then provided for each weak verb type, illustrating how these verbs appear in real Quranic contexts. The lesson reinforces recognition of conjugational patterns — especially stem changes in hollow, defective, and lafīf verbs across all pronouns.

name.en: Doubly Weak Verb (Lafīf) — Past Tense Conjugation
Doubled and Hamzated Verb — Past Tense Conjugation

verified_title.en: Defective Verbs: Full Conjugation Tables (Imperfect, Imperative, Negation with لَنْ and لا)
verified_title.tr: İlletli Fiiller: Tam Çekim Tabloları (Muzari, Emir, لَنْ ve لا ile Olumsuzluk)
verified_title.ar: الأفعال المعتلة: جداول التصريف الكاملة (مضارع، أمر، النفي بلَنْ ولا)

L3W5.summary: This lesson presents comprehensive conjugation tables for all major verb types in Arabic — sound (salim), hamzated (mahmuz), doubled (mudaaf), assimilated (mithal), hollow (ajwaf), defective (naqis), and doubly weak (lafif) — across imperfect, imperative, dual, and negated forms. The tables cover all pronouns including dual forms and feminine plural. Quranic example verses illustrate the defective and weak verb forms in context.

name.tr: Nakıs Fiil (son harfi illet olan fiil)
Ecvef Fiil (orta harfi illet olan fiil)
Misal Fiil (ilk harfi illet olan fiil)
Lefif Fiil (iki harfi illet olan fiil)

name.ar: النفي بـ لَنْ ولا الناهية مع الأفعال المعتلة
name.en: Negation with لَنْ and Prohibitive لا for Weak Verbs
name.tr: İlletli Fiillerde لَنْ ve Nehiy لا ile Olumsuzluk

verified_title.en: Conditional Sentences and Conditional Particles in Arabic
verified_title.tr: Şart Cümlesi ve Şart Edatları

L3W6.summary: This lesson introduces the Arabic conditional sentence (الجملة الشرطية), explaining that it is a compound sentence consisting of a condition clause (جملة الشرط) and a response clause (جملة جواب الشرط) linked by a conditional particle. It systematically covers jazm-inducing (جازمة) conditional particles such as إنْ, مَن, ما, مهما, متى, أين, كيفما, and أيّ, as well as non-jazm-inducing (غير جازمة) particles such as لو, لولا, أمّا, إذا, لمّا, and كلّما. The lesson also details the various forms the response clause (جواب الشرط) can take, including nominal sentences, negation with ما/لن/لا, imperative verbs, and particles like قد, السين, سوف, and كأنّما.

examples: مَنْ جَاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثَالِهَا
لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَذَا الْقُرْآنَ عَلَى جَبَلٍ
فَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ مَا عَرَفُوا كَفَرُوا بِهِ
وَإِذَا مَسَّ النَّاسَ ضُرٌّ دَعَوْا رَبَّهُمْ
وَلَوْ شَاءَ رَبُّكَ لَجَعَلَ النَّاسَ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً

name.en: Response Clause of the Conditional and Its Connectors

examples: فَإِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَمَا سَأَلْتُكُمْ مِنْ أَجْرٍ
فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْرًا يَرَهُ

verified_title.en: Circumstantial Accusative (Hal) and Specification (Tamyiz)
verified_title.tr: Hal ve Temyiz

L3W7.summary: This lesson introduces two accusative constructions in Arabic grammar: Hal (circumstantial accusative), which describes the state or condition of a noun at the time of an action and agrees with its referent in gender and number; and Tamyiz (specification/distinction), which is an indefinite noun that clarifies vagueness in either a quantity/measure (tamyiz al-dhat) or a sentence-level relationship (tamyiz al-nisbah). Numerous Quranic examples from surahs including al-Hujurat, Yusuf, al-Baqarah, and others are used to illustrate both constructions.

name.en: Agreement of Hal with its referent (sahib al-hal)

examples: رَبَّنَا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَّحْمَةً وَعِلْمًا

verified_title.en: Arabic Cardinal Numbers (al-ʿAdad wa-l-Maʿdūd) and Apposition (al-Badal)
verified_title.tr: Arapça Sayılar (el-Aded ve'l-Ma'dûd) ve Bedel

L3W8.summary: This lesson covers Arabic cardinal numbers from 1 to 1000+, including gender polarity rules (mukhalafa) for numbers 3–10, compound numbers 11–19, tens, hundreds, and thousands, along with the grammatical case of the counted noun (maʿdūd) at each range. It also introduces fractions (al-kusūr) such as ½, ⅓, ¼ etc. and the distributive forms (mathnā, thulātha, rubāʿ). Additionally, the lesson teaches the grammatical concept of apposition (al-badal), covering its three types: badal kull min kull (complete substitution), badal baʿḍ min kull (partial substitution), and badal ishtimāl (associative apposition), with Quranic examples for each.

name.en: Cardinal Numbers and the Counted Noun

name.ar: مخالفة العدد للمعدود في التذكير والتأنيث

name.tr: Soru/Belirsiz Nicelik Bildiren كَمْ ve كَأَيِّن

examples: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الشَّهْرِ الْحَرَامِ قِتَالٍ فِيهِ

verified_title.en: Diptote Nouns (Al-Mamnu' min al-Sarf / Ghayrul Munsarif)
verified_title.tr: Gayri Munsarif İsimler (Ğayrul Munsarif)

L3W9.summary: This lesson teaches the concept of diptote nouns (al-mamnu' min al-sarf / ghayrul munsarif) — declinable nouns that do not accept tanwin. It explains that such nouns take damma in the nominative, but fatha (instead of kasra) in both the accusative and genitive cases, except when preceded by the definite article 'al-' or used in an idafa construction, in which case they revert to kasra. The lesson catalogues the grammatical categories that trigger diptote status, including broken plurals of specific patterns, feminine alif endings, proper nouns with various features, adjectives on the af'alu pattern, and more, illustrating each with Quranic examples.

name.en: Broken Plural of Mafa'il / Mafa'il Pattern

name.tr: Uzatılmış ve Kısaltılmış Müennes Elif Eki

verified_title.en: Declinable & Indeclinable Words (Mu'rab & Mabni) and Relative Pronouns (Ism al-Mawsul)
verified_title.tr: Mebni ve Mu'rab Kelimeler ile İsm-i Mevsul

L3W10.summary: This lesson distinguishes between declinable words (al-mu'rab), whose endings change according to grammatical position, and indeclinable words (al-mabni), whose endings remain fixed. It covers six categories of indeclinable nouns (pronouns, demonstratives, relative pronouns, interrogative nouns, conditional nouns, and verbal nouns), the declension of verbs, and a full declension table for nouns. The second half of the lesson is dedicated to relative pronouns (al-ism al-mawsul), presenting all forms (الذي، التي، اللذان، اللتان، الذين، اللاتي، من، ما) with their usage conditions and Quranic examples.

name.en: Declinable and Indeclinable Words (Mu'rab and Mabni)
Noun Declension (singular, dual, sound plural, broken plural)

current_title.tr: Mebnî ve Mu'rab Kelimeler / İsmi Mevsul

verified_title.en: Exception (Istithna) and Absolute Object (Maf'ul Mutlaq)
verified_title.tr: İstisna ve Mef'ul Mutlak

L3W11.summary: This lesson covers the grammar of exception (al-istithna'), explaining the particles of exception (إلا، غير، سوى، خلا، عدا، حاشا), the case endings of the excepted noun in affirmative and negative sentences, and the distinction between إلا as an exception particle versus أَلا and أَلَّا as other particles. It then introduces the absolute object (maf'ul mutlaq), defined as a verbal noun (masdar) mentioned after a verb of the same root to emphasize meaning, specify number, or clarify type, with Quranic examples illustrating all uses.

name.en: Absolute Object Substituting for its Verb

verified_title.en: Emphasis (Tawkid): Emphatic Lam, Emphatic Nun (Nun al-Tawkid al-Thaqila and Khafifa)
verified_title.tr: Te'kid: Te'kid Lamı, Te'kid Nunu (Ağır ve Hafif Nun)
verified_title.ar: التوكيد: لام التوكيد ونونا التوكيد الثقيلة والخفيفة

L3W12.summary: This lesson introduces the major tools of emphasis (tawkid) in Arabic, including inna/anna, lam al-ibtida (the lam mazhlaqah), lam al-qasam, the particle qad, and the maf'ul mutlaq. The central focus is on the emphatic nun (nun al-tawkid al-thaqila and al-khafifa), which attaches to the present tense (mudari') and imperative (amr) verbs to convey strong affirmation. The lesson details the grammatical conditions under which the emphatic nun is obligatory, permissible, or prohibited, and provides conjugation tables and Quranic examples throughout.

current_title.tr: Te'kid: Te'kid Lamı, Te'kid Nunu, نَّ

verified_title.en: Augmented (Derived) Verb Forms: Triliteral with One, Two, and Three Added Letters (Forms II–V)
verified_title.tr: Mezid Fiiller: Bir, İki ve Üç Harfle Türetilmiş Fiil Kalıpları (II–V. Bablar)
verified_title.ar: الفعل المزيد الثلاثي بحرف وبحرفين وبثلاثة أحرف (الأبواب الثاني حتى الخامس)

L3W13.summary: This lesson introduces the distinction between bare (mujarrad) and augmented (mazid) triliteral verbs, then systematically presents four augmented verb forms: فَعَّلَ (Form II, one added letter – gemination), أَفْعَلَ (Form IV, one added letter – prefixed hamza), فَاعَلَ (Form III, one added letter – lengthened first vowel), تَفَعَّلَ (Form V), and تَفَاعَلَ (Form VI). For each pattern, the lesson provides the full paradigm including past tense, imperfect, imperative, active participle, passive participle, and verbal noun (masdar), along with numerous Quranic examples illustrating each form in context.

name.en: Form II verb (faʿʿala) – geminated middle radical
name.tr: II. Bab (feʿʿale) – orta harf şeddeli fiil
IV. Bab (efʿale) – başına hemze eklenen fiil

name.en: Form III verb (fāʿala) – lengthened first vowel (alif after first radical)
name.tr: III. Bab (fāʿale) – birinci harften sonra elif uzatmalı fiil

name.en: Form V verb (tafaʿʿala) – prefixed ta + geminated middle radical
name.tr: V. Bab (tefaʿʿale) – başında ta ve şeddeli orta harf

name.en: Form VI verb (tafāʿala) – prefixed ta + alif after first radical
name.tr: VI. Bab (tefāʿale) – başında ta ve elif uzatmalı fiil

name.en: Bare (mujarrad) vs. augmented (mazid) verb – root classification
name.tr: Mücerred ve Mezid Fiil Ayrımı – Kök Sınıflandırması

current_title.ar: الأفعال المزيدة — الأبواب II إلى V

verified_title.en: Derived Verb Forms (Augmented Triliteral and Quadriliteral): Forms VII, VIII, X, and Quadriliteral Patterns
verified_title.tr: Türetilmiş Fiil Kalıpları: VII., VIII., X. Bablar ve Dörtlü Kök Fiiller
verified_title.ar: الفعل المجرد والفعل المزيد: الأبواب السابع والثامن والعاشر والرباعي المزيد

L3W14.summary: This lesson presents the conjugation paradigms for Arabic augmented verb forms VII (انفعل), VIII (افتعل), and X (استفعل), including their past tense, present tense, imperative, active participle, passive participle, and verbal noun forms. It also introduces quadriliteral verb patterns (فعللة) and their augmented derivatives (تفعلل، افعنلل، افعللّ). Each pattern is illustrated with multiple Quranic examples.

name.en: Augmented quadriliteral verb forms (tafa'lala, if'anlala, if'alalla)
name.tr: Artırılmış Dörtlü Fiil Kalıpları (tefe'lele, if'enlele, if'ellele)

name.ar: اسم الفاعل واسم المفعول والمصدر للأبواب المزيدة
name.en: Active participle, passive participle, and verbal noun of augmented forms
name.tr: Artırılmış Fiil Kalıplarında İsm-i Fail, İsm-i Mef'ul ve Mastar

current_title.ar: الأفعال المزيدة — الأبواب VI إلى X
